summary: Gruber reviews two third-party iPhone camera apps that challenge the default iPhone camera's processing philosophy. Not Boring's !Camera uses LUTs for film-like color grading and a 'SuperRAW' format that embraces sensor grain rather than smoothing it away, producing ready-to-share images without post-processing. Adobe's Project Indigo, led by former Google Pixel camera chief Marc Levoy, takes a different approach by capturing and merging up to 32 frames with minimal tone mapping and sharpening to produce natural-looking photos rivaling dedicated cameras. Gruber notes that Apple deliberately makes different trade-offs in its built-in Camera app — prioritizing speed and battery life — which is correct for a default app but leaves room for these more deliberate alternatives. He personally gravitates toward !Camera for its fun, zero-editing workflow, while acknowledging Indigo's extraordinary results for those willing to invest the time.
key_points: Not Boring's !Camera uses professional 3D LUTs and a SuperRAW format that embraces natural sensor grain instead of smoothing it, producing film-like ready-to-share images

Adobe's Project Indigo captures up to 32 frames per photo with minimal processing to achieve natural-looking results that rival large-sensor dedicated cameras, but at the cost of speed and battery life

Apple's built-in Camera app makes deliberate trade-offs favoring speed and battery efficiency, which is correct for a default app but opens the door for third-party alternatives with different priorities
Fun and whimsy in tool design is a vastly underestimated attribute — !Camera's playful appearance masks genuinely serious functionality
Marc Levoy, who led Google Pixel's acclaimed computational photography, is now applying that expertise at Adobe with Indigo
money_quotes: The fact that it's fun makes me want to use it — a vastly underestimated attribute of tool design.
!Camera looks gimmicky but I assure you it's not — and what might strike you as gimmicky is really just plain fun and whimsical.
Rather than fighting the nature of the small (and thus, noisy) sensors in the iPhone camera systems, SuperRAW processing embraces the noise, imbuing images with natural-looking grain.
Slow and battery-draining is exactly why Apple hasn't pursued these sorts of advanced computational photography techniques in the built-in Camera app.
Apple is making entirely different trade-offs — correctly — for the default Camera app.
Pro and prosumer photographers may want to make completely different trade-offs when it comes to image processing time and energy.
If they hadn't told me, I'd have wagered their photos were taken with dedicated large-sensor digital cameras, not phones.
key_insight: The best iPhone camera apps succeed not by fighting the iPhone's hardware limitations but by making different philosophical trade-offs than Apple's default — embracing grain, slowing down capture, or applying professional color science — and the fact that fun is a legitimate design goal for serious tools is vastly underappreciated.
